speaker: interviewer
text: --married Amato. I'm the interviewer, Mrs. Rebecca [? Ziman, ?] born [? Cone. ?] Tape five. Madame Lucia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please explain a little bit. We've got some postcards and some photographs in between here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting on the left where the left picture is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Rhodes Island, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is that one? Can you show us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That one here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we used to go there and all the weddings they used to [? perform ?] for months there, you know, the weddings. And this one is also the same [? shul. ?] My daughter went to this 1996 and they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter, Jeanette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeanette, this picture. And here-- here is Rhodes Island in [INAUDIBLE] and this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: This one here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, La [? Cancha. ?] Where-- my aunt used to live there with my uncle. And there it was-- he had also the cousin, [INAUDIBLE], you know, Italian. It was the [INAUDIBLE]. The-- you see that hall there?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is all Rhodes Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Rhodes Island. And you want this one? This is where they used to leave all these yachts, you know. And people would-- they used to walk up and down in the [INAUDIBLE], you see. And everybody used to stop to see the waterfront, you know. It was nice, very nice. And there is where-- is [INAUDIBLE], they call [? La ?] [? Sierra ?]. We used to-- when we came-- went to the concentration camp, the-- the Germans, they took us this road from the [? Mandrate, ?] they took us there and took us to the port where their boat was there and we embarked to go to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually walked along this road here--

speaker: interviewee
text: This road, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans captured you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this one is also on the roads, you know. All the little villages, you see, like [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and-- and this-- this is the statue. What is the name that one? Can't even see.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were lovely villages there, you see. And they-- we used to go for-- for a drive with the-- with the bus, you know, all the time because everybody, now-- they didn't have a car, you know. Those [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], how do you call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], with the horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cart and horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Or sometimes we used to walk with the bike, [NON-ENGLISH]. Very often there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you liked Rhodes Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really nice, you know. I-- I thought I'd never move from that country because it was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. There is too-- too many visitors, too many holidaymakers there, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a bit of a nightmare when you had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you went back to Rhodes after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went 1962 with my husband. I went in 1963 to Italy, but I never went to Rhodes. So when my husband came, 1962, we went and we stopped there for a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression when you arrived in Rhodes?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice. Oh, I cried. And all the people, they knew me, the Greek people, the Turkish people. They asked me where is my other sister, my other brothers, and I told them they were dead, you know. And they were crying. They asked me [? to ?] [? go ?]. They gave me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were nice to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice, very nice. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were heart sore when you went to Rhodes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children they like it very much. They used to swim there. 1962, Jeanette was still [INAUDIBLE], and I took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rhodes Island. And we were happy all the time there. And the neighbors, they-- it was like all the family, big family in-- on Rhodes Island. Everybody likes us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a lovely picture here of a building. Could you just tell me where this--

speaker: interviewee
text: This--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Rhodes Island where my house used to be there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The-- the-- that one, it-- [POINTS] it was the entrance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That one. And then we used to live where is the balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the lounge up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we-- we used to live, my-- myself and my three sisters and one brother plus my father. And after he died, then we used to live the four of us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was your mom also living in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we used to live in the house, but the other-- had another house inside. What another flat, what you call? Because that one, when my mother died, we didn't want to live in the same house. We changed and we went up. See there is a double story that--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived downstairs before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you moved upstairs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were really happy there and then, you know, after my father died in 1942. So we used to be the four of us to live there. And all the neighbors, they were very nice to us and my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of-- Do you remember the name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that in the center of Rhodes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were quite central?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were near the sea also and the [NON-ENGLISH], you know? What do you call the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: The walls. The walls, you know? Soldiers used put the-- [? in the area ?] there to-- to shoot the airplanes, you know. We used to live there. And-- and next here, there were another balcony, used to live [? Estella ?] [? Israel ?] who was a [INAUDIBLE] before. She also went to camp with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the father died also. And the mother and herself, they were alone also.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you have a lot of memories when you see this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see-- Yes, because my daughter went 1985 to Rhodes and then she took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you also went to Rhodes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: We took other pictures too, but I don't-- I don't have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you go inside the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression when you went in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because now somebody was living there, you know. They said come in, come in, you're welcome. They gave us nice coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel a little bit [INAUDIBLE]. They told me where are-- where is your brother? Where is-- and I say they are dead and everything and they were very upset. And many people, they change also. They left from [? there. ?] There were many Jewish and three or four families only Greek, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that must have been very emotional when you went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I used to cry, you know. All these streets I used to go walking and they used to come and look. Hello, hello, Lucia. Lucia-- they used to call me 'Luchia,' Lucia in Greek, you know. They were very pleased to see me alive. They call you survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel better that she went back to look at the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Better and sad, you know. And with the children and then they-- they used talk to me and then all of the sudden I used to talk in Greek. And I used to talk to the children in Greek, but it was the language [LAUGHS] in English to talk to them. You know, I was confused, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were confused when you went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia, there is a lovely picture here. Can you tell us where it's been taken if you remember when and who is on this lovely picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me but in Rhodes Island when it was Purim. Maybe I was 12 or 13 years old, you know. So my-- my sister had it in Italy so when my daughter went for a holiday, she gave it to her because I didn't have no pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you used to dress up for Purim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We used to have fun, you know, going to the neighbors to get money. [LAUGHS] It was-- you know, when you were young, you want money, you want sweets. So I look big there, and taller.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us who this picture is, Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my two sisters with my mom, you know, in Rhodes Island. This is Djoya is the eldest sister and Rina is the-- she was going to have a baby and my mother in the-- in the middle. After my sister had a baby, you know, 1934-- '35, my mother died in 1936. And this picture, my sister had it and Jeanette brought them from Rome. And there is very little picture. My daughter made it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Enlarged.

speaker: interviewee
text: Enlarged to make the picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is the one that was married to the Italian?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two of them, they married the Italian. This one, she came to the concentration camp. She lives in Italy, in [INAUDIBLE]. And she lives in Rome-- she was-- she used to live in Rome. She died also and she left four children. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they both married out of faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of faith, yeah. And then my sister now lives in-- in Italy, the one she came from the concentration camp and she had one daughter, Rosa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents upset when they married Italians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were a little bit upset, but when-- when she-- she married only that one she married before, you see, he was Italian. She married after the war with somebody help her, you know. So they fell in love, you see? [INAUDIBLE]. They got married and they were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's 20 years. She is widow [INAUDIBLE] because the husband had the bronchitis. He was-- he was also like a-- a policeman, you see, and was in the night out, I don't know. They had an accident and then he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did this one have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, three daughters and one son. They live in Rome now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them in 1962, but the one came here to see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, before my husband died.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had them for six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember all their names, of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names? Yes. I know the-- the children from my sister, Rina. The first was Bernardina the second, Mario, the third one, Rosina, and the fourth, Juliana. And my sister, Djoya had only one daughter, Rosemarie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she is the eldest sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she the the one that you were helping to feed in the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I wanted, she must live because the other one, she died there. So I wanted to-- to live her and I was helping her, everything. And now when-- when she phoned me from Italy, she said, look after yourself, don't cry, and lose weight and walk and make gymnastics. You must pray to God because he is the one to help us. She said to me on the telephone maybe two weeks ago I spoke to her. And she said, [NON-ENGLISH], I'm so pleased to hear your voice because she worries when I'm-- when I tell her I'm not well, you see. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you speak to her often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She-- I brought her here. I sent the ticket, she was here. 11 years ago, she came. She was with us two months, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your mom? Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom, she was really nice lady, you know. Always she used to give us nice food. It was nice to see them in the table and with all the children, you know. But she used to prefer me because-- because when she fell sick, and I used to work in the factory, you know, like I told you for cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she used to send me food sometimes. So that day when she died, before she died in the afternoon, she said to my sister, you send Lucia food. It was Saturday, I used to work extra. So he send me-- she sent me eggs with tomato. You know, tomato. You-- you make the tomato and then after you break the eggs. It was very nice. But she knew I didn't like the tomato alone. She used to strain them and then put the eggs in. And she sent it with my brother, my brother, Maurizio He came with the bike, and he brought me the lunch. I said, how is mommy? [? She's ?] [? sick, ?] is not so well. So by the time I came 6 o' clock from work, she was dead. That day she was thinking about me and sent me the last food, you know. So and then I was crying a lot for my mother. And then it was my father was old because my father was older than my mother, 20 years, you know. He-- he was older 20 years. My mother, she was very young when she got married. He was also very nice, and he was friend of your father. He used come always. They used to drink [INAUDIBLE], you know, this [INAUDIBLE]. And he used to wear a fez. My father used to wear the fez [INAUDIBLE], you know, like the Turkish people. Yeah, what can you do? Did you ever meet your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only-- I had only one granny I knew. She died in early '90. It was my mother's mother. And her name, it was Kaden, Clara, like-- yeah. And she was very nice. When I used to get cross with my sister, she-- I used to go and cry there, you know, because I had an auntie, the other sister of my mother who lived with my granny, you see. She looked-- she used to look after the granny and then I used to go there. She said don't cry, don't cry because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your granny's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaden. Yeah. She was very nice. Only one granny, I never saw grandpas, nothing. They were all dead, you see. But still after they died, you know, you cry, you cry, and then you forget all about, and you live your life. And we were happy again, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since-- and then when they-- when the Germans came, they took us, and I used to cry and say I worked so hard. I had few things for me from my [? dresser ?]. We left everything, and we lost everything. And we went to suffer there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to cry every time. I say, well, [INAUDIBLE] we are going to leave, you know. So when I went to Rome [INAUDIBLE] with my sister, it was not so bad. And then everybody was going immigrating to the Congo, to the America. And they used to say, where are you going? I say, where can I go? I am not going nowhere because I haven't got brothers and sisters [? living out. ?] So my husband, he was a neighbor of-- on Rhodes Island and he knew me. He was thinking-- in the letter he sent me he said he was thinking one day to come and get married with me, but the chances because mother and father and the two brothers then his sister, that they were cremated also in the concentration camp. So he asked me in the letter, how I survivor, and then, don't-- he said, don't worry. You-- we are going to get married and we live happily. You know? All the letters that he used to send me, he used to send them with a heart, you know. He used to draw. The-- the-- the-- one heart to live with-- one heart with two lives, you know. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He used to send me such lovely letters. I used to go to the [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you say [NON-ENGLISH] in English? Embassy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Embassy, American embassy. And that lady she said, oh you lucky girl, you are the [? first. ?] You are going Rhodesia. I used to show all the letters I used to get from him. My husband, he was an [? engineer ?], you know. He was clever in school and used to take the books in every-- in every class. I showed you just now a book with a [INAUDIBLE]. Bring the thing. And I've got the diploma, you know? All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something else about your mom? Or do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I miss her very much, you know. But always, the mothers always, they worry about the children. Like me, I worry about my two children. And I don't want nothing to happen. When they've got-- they've got something, they worry so much. I said, better to come to me, not to them, you know? Like I said to Jeanette always.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when your mom died?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14, 15, something like this, yeah-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my dad died-- died after from [INAUDIBLE]. My father died in 1942, May 10. I remember May, June-- I think June. How many years is it now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Many.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, she used to prepare nice food for us and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I used to help to plate-- to-- to wash the plates, you see, or like it was Pesach, like, and we used to wash all the dishes again. And-- and we used to have other plates for Pesach, but the-- the dishes, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The pots.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pots and things, we used to boil them with [? sand ?] and things to make them kosher, you know, for Pesach. But the plates and the cups we used to have separate, you see. And my mother, she used to smoke all week, but Saturday she was a little bit nervous because she couldn't smoke so she used to take [? pitas, ?] you know? Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Pips. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Pips ?] to eat to pass the time, you see. She never played cards, my mother, and she was very pleasant. And she used to sew for us, you know, making dresses, lovely little dresses with my sister, Djoya, used to help her, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work in the same business as your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father used to work alone. I told you he used to sell fruit, dried fruits, [INAUDIBLE], all the things, you know, almonds toasted, everything of this. You see? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My mother, she had been [INAUDIBLE] because during the war, the Greeks removed all the cemeteries, and they made them all gardens. So my mother, she still-- she was still fresh and they took-- they show us. It was all bones, you know, not quite finished. And they-- they put them together, all the things, because they mixed. So the made a very big [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Very big grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they are there, you see, but my father got the grave because that cemetery, it was [NON-ENGLISH] for many years. The Greeks, they want to make gardens.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you see all this? When you went over?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they actually open up for you to see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, weren't you shocked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What can you do? Say this is a thing like this they did and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia, this is a lovely picture. Does it bring you memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, plenty, you know. I had-- that time I had a lot of hair, you know, and they were curly, nice hair. I used to make [INAUDIBLE], but this time when I made this picture, I put it all back. It doesn't look like me now. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it-- what do you remember when you see a picture like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, look, I was so lovely. But always they used to say to me, you are very pretty girl, you know, and the people they used to come and call me for making-- [INAUDIBLE] advertising, you know, for my teeth, but now-- [LAUGHS] I'm old now, you see. And this picture is also my sister had them, you know, so she gave it to [INAUDIBLE], to Jeanette.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember much from that age--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you used to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to-- all day, we used to walk to the beach, walking and dancing and singing. And still I was at school at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but 17, 18, maybe. Yeah. My husband was already-- it was-- [INAUDIBLE]. He was in Rhodesia already. Maybe it was 1938. He-- when my mother died, he was in Rhodes Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he remembers you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As neighbors--

speaker: interviewee
text: Neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: From that age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He's [INAUDIBLE], he say we used to play doctor and nurse. [LAUGHTER] It's true. We used to play. Silica, somebody of 35 and Jeanette, she's 45, they play together, they go out together, and it doesn't look at the time you are older, you know. It's something-- we were five years older than me, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very happy picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, myself, you know. Here it's opposite our home. It was the [? mura. ?] And always we used to sit there in the sand and doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Embroidery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Embroidery. And all the-- my neighbors, they used to sit in that place. That day, somebody passed through and the man, they made this picture. You see, it is not a photograph, it is just a little picture, you know. Yeah. You like it? Nice?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still in that time and jumping. My husband say, I met you because you used to-- to go in the trees and-- I was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Agile.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like [INAUDIBLE], I used to be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So. This dress, I remember. When I used to put it it was really nice. It was a-- it is the color of vinegar, you know, not brown, but it was such a lovely-- this material, it was sended by my auntie from America. so-- the dressmaker made-- made me that dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made it for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dressmaker, Jewish girl. She also died in-- in the bomb. [INAUDIBLE], you know [INAUDIBLE], the sister, [INAUDIBLE]. Her auntie, that was a dressmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this picture taken just before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- Yeah, before, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the year it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, '39, maybe, before. I was so happy there. I had boyfriends and things. Yeah. And I was engaged to somebody in Rhodes, but he came to the concentration camp and he died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac Gallanti. He had a mother and two sisters. Also married them, they had children all-- all burned there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, yeah. No survivors in that family or the two brothers and the sisters too. And with children, and the one, she was married, and she-- she was expecting. Shame. Yeah. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you got engaged?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this fellow? About-- about 20-something, I guess, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that lovely actress, Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when you-- you know, when you're thin, you look tall and with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the high heeled shoes, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk a little bit about the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made this picture for the passport because we didn't have passport. We were making one. And-- and I-- I had them this made. So now my daughter, she made it in big.

speaker: interviewer
text: She enlarged it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- she enlarge it because to make this picture, what you said, the video.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, and then when I was in Italy my husband knew me from-- I must talk about [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so I was in Italy, and then all of a sudden I got a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got a letter and my brother said to me, give me some present because I brought a letter from the Congo, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Zimbabwe.

speaker: interviewee
text: South Africa, from Zimbabwe. So I gave him 50 cents, I remember, [INAUDIBLE]. And I got the letter, and he asked me how I-- my husband wrote me a letter. It was telling me he met my cousin and say we came back from the concentration camp. And he gave the address, and he wrote to me. And he said, how you [? survive? ?] Because he had lost all the family there. And now it's 11 years I don't see you, from 19-- he left 1937 and 1947. Plus-- he say, 11 years I don't see you. You're still pretty. Send me a picture. So myself, I had a pictures, the small one. I send one. And then the other letter he-- he asked me to get-- to marry him.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so happy. And everyone said, oh, you lucky girl, you lucky girl, you-- you're taking an [? engineer, ?] you know? [LAUGHS] So and then I was so happy. By the time I came, it past another year, you know. We got engaged 1940-- '47, yeah. Seven months, eight months I came to get married. And we were very happy. And everybody will see this picture because my daughter, I don't know where she put the small one. You could see the same one, but she made it in big, you know. You like it, [? Sean ?]?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very good. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's lovely. You say that some Greek people wanted to photograph you for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To take of-- for the [INAUDIBLE] to make an advertisement for their toothpaste, you know. So my mother-- at the time my mother said, no, you can't go into this because if you did all these things in the [INAUDIBLE] it was like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Scandal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So once I did, they put you in the paper, you know, the newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a pity. Madame Lucia, could you please tell us who is on the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me again, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back from the concentration camp, and I went-- I was with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after the concentration camp do you think it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were already, you can see, they were growing out my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe after six, seven months, something, after I came from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When you're young, you always look nice. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You put on weight very quickly then.

speaker: interviewee
text: There? Yeah. My sister, she used to beat me every morning an egg, you know, and give me with milk, you know, and sugar, [? they took ?] the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to make--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you recovered quite quickly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you forget about the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all the time I was telling, I was singing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you telling stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Songs. Yeah. And then for two years, I was in that village, you know, with my sister, by the time they came. When I arrived here, 1947, to Rhodesia, they left also the-- the village and they went to live in Austria because their children, they were growing, and they wanted big schools, you know, something like this. They left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us more about the picture? Does it bring you memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Yeah, in the village--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually write a story straight after you came out of the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, no, I didn't write nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you try and forget?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, always I like-- I liked to tell the people I was in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I see many people and they ask me, oh where do you come from? Because you speak a different language. They ask me if I'm Italian. I say, yes, I'm an Italian Jew. And I was in Auschwitz. Say, you? And I showed my number always, and they say very proud-- you must be proud because you came-- you saved yourself, you know. She said, [INAUDIBLE] all this is true, what happened? I say yes. So how you survive? I say, I don't know. I was working, and the war was finished, and we were young.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression when you first saw-- when-- what actually happened when-- at the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were crying. We-- w e we're liberated, now it's finished, the war. You know? We were crying, we were singing, we were dancing. We used to take these chairs playing with the [? Matilda ?] and [INAUDIBLE], all these people, you know. We were so happy. And then all of a sudden we divide again. Many people left to go to the brothers and, you know, and sisters. And we remained in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think that you were going to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't know but always I had faith in God, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't really blame God for having suffered?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because this is life, you see, and once I'm happy because God help me to survive and to have a nice life after. Because it was a lady, [INAUDIBLE] mother, you know, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE]. She said don't cry, Lucia, don't cry. You'll see then you'll-- you'll-- you'll find something happy, you know, how I knew I was going to be alive. And then what she told me, sometimes when I seen her, I say your mother told me so and so, and it's true. You see, you must have faith in God. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a beautiful picture. Who is the person on it. This is my husband, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe, [? Moises, ?] they used to call him [? Moises ?], Amato. So he sent me one like this, a small one. When-- and I show-- oh-- everybody in the village. When I show this picture, they say, oh what a lovely man.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean after the liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, after I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947 When I got engaged, he sent me this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got-- you got engaged by letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: By letter, I was engaged by letter. And all the letters he used to send me, oh love, love, because he said he was thinking of-- of me all the time. They gave him other girls here in Rhodesia. He didn't want to-- to get engaged with other people. He say, I must go and get Lucia because he loved me from long time, but he didn't say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the mother and father, they were very nice to me because we were neighbors. I used to go, and I used to have lunches, dinners, or the sister [INAUDIBLE] fruit. We were like a-- a relation. So when I got engaged, the father and mother all dead. They died in the concentration camp. That's your husband's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the family, and all the family. Now he's-- he was the only survivor. And he had one brother in Israel so we brought him to Rhodesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your husband didn't go to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he came 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in Rhodesia-- He was in Rhodesia already.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1937, he came to Rhodesia like bookkeeper, you see. And he was working for 11 years for the cousin. So after we got married, and I had a baby, and then I say, it's time. You are clever. You know everything. So I better look for you-- for a shop. So he went to look for a shop, and he bought the shop. So he-- he left-- he gave notice to the cousin. And we worked together after. I didn't know the language, English, and he wanted to teach me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I learned a few things in-- in German in-- in the concentration camp. Like I say, good night, and then he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH] night. And I said goodnight in English. And [INAUDIBLE] he said, no you don't learn. I say, I don't want to learn English because [LAUGHS] all the time when I used to talk, I didn't say properly good morning. It was what good [? morning ?], you know, like them. So-- and then one day I say all right. But he teach me straight away in one week I learned the black language, you know. And I knew already-- The African language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the African language. But the English it took me a little bit longer. So now I-- I spoke and I know a little bit of English too because we didn't study English, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you had a choice to do the interview in Ladino or in English but--
